source_metadata.title_raw: Ash Pirayou - I-20-025 - April 4, 2020 - Costa Mesa

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 14, 2020

Ash Pirayou
Rutan & Tucker, LLP
611 Anton Blvd, Suite 1400
Costa Mesa, CA 92626

Re:     Your Request for Informal Assistance
        Our File No. I-20-025

Dear Mr. Pirayou:

        This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Boardmember Gary Kremen of
the Santa Clara Valley Water District regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest
provisions of the Act and not under other conflict of interest laws, such as common law conflict of
interest. Because your request involves general guidance not limited to a specific decision, we are
treating it as one for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(b)(6)(F). Informal assistance
does not provide the requester with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) In addition, we do not provide informal assistance on
Section 1090 issues.² We recommend that Boardmember Kremen seek advice prior to participating
in a specific decision that involves any of his financial interests.

QUESTION

        Do the conflict of interest provisions under the Act prohibit Boardmember Kremen from
having an interest in WaterSmart Software, LLC, including possibly serving on its board, while
simultaneously serving on the board of the Santa Clara Valley Water District ("Water District")?

CONCLUSION

        As outlined below, the Act would prohibit Boardmember Kremen from taking part in Water
District decisions that have a foreseeable material financial effect on his potential interests in
WaterSmart. However, this is not a determination we can make absent the specific facts of the

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² As required by state law we forwarded your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the
Attorney General's Office and the Santa Clara County District Attorney's Office. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not
receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for
purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other
than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

File No. I-20-025
Page No. 2

decision. Generally, we can only caution that the Act may prohibit him from taking part in such
decisions if he has a potentially disqualifying economic interest under the Act.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

Gary Kremen is an elected board member of the Water District.³ He represents the City of
Palo Alto, the City of Mountain View, the City of Los Altos, the Town of Los Altos Hills, the City
of Monte Sereno, the Town of Los Gatos, parts of the City of San Jose and parts of unincorporated
Santa Clara County.

The Water District

The Water District provides water on a wholesale basis to the 2.1 million people in Santa
Clara County through 15 water retailers and to well owners within Santa Clara County. Water
retailers are entities that sell water directly to individual households, individual commercial
customers, individual industrial customers, and individual agricultural customers. Examples of
water retailers are the City of Mountain View, the San Jose Water Company and the City of San
Jose. The Water District is not considered a water retailer; it only sells a de minimis amount of
water to individual households, individual commercial customers, individual industrial customers,
and individual agricultural customers.

The Water District also provides flood control and creek ecosystem stewardship within
Santa Clara County. The Water District Board also makes decisions on a number of water-related
issues. Typical issues voted on by the Board include: 1) whether the Water District should institute
water banking; 2) the commitment of the Water District for the percent usage of the Delta
Conveyance tunnel; 3) whether the Water District should enter into a construction agreement, a
consulting agreement, or a partnership involving wholesale water delivery, flood control, or
ecosystems restorations; 4) whether the Water District should defend challenges to its local water
rights; 5) whether the Water District should enter into a construction contract; 6) whether the Water
District should approve a consulting contract; and 7) the amount the Water District should pay its
employees.

WaterSmart Software, LLC

Boardmember Kremen has an ownership stake in a public benefit company formed in 2009
called WaterSmart Software, LLC ("WaterSmart"). Public benefit companies are usually called B-
Corporations, which are a hybrid of non-profit and for-profit entities. Unlike strict for-profit
corporations, B-Corporations consider factors other than profits when making decisions, including
the needs of stakeholders such as employees, vendors, suppliers, and the environment.

Boardmember Kremen currently has less than a 1 percent ownership interest in WaterSmart.
In fact, because of the money invested by subsequent individuals and entities in WaterSmart,
Boardmember Kremen believes his current stake is worthless. He previously served on the
WaterSmart Board of Directors but resigned before being elected to the Water District Board in
2014. He may have a desire to rejoin the WaterSmart Board of Directors in the future, as well as

³ See http://www.valleywater.org.

File No. I-20-025
Page No. 3

make a future investment in WaterSmart. If he does rejoin the board of WaterSmart, he will not
receive any income; however, if WaterSmart is ever sold, he would receive income from the sale.

WaterSmart is a software company that provides technology solutions to water retailers,
allowing the retailers "to better communicate with their consumers about the value of water, how
their water use compares to others, and how they can save money. WaterSmart's suite of
engagement and analytics tools allow customers to help themselves by accessing detailed
information to answer questions and solve common problems. These capabilities translate into
significant cost savings for utilities, help protect revenue, and create dramatic improvements in
customer satisfaction levels. This all contributes to more resilient water systems that benefit
customers, the communities they live in, and the environment."⁴

The aspect of WaterSmart's business relevant to this discussion is its ability to
"communicate with their consumers about the value of water, how their water use compares to
others, and how they can save money." Broadly, this is referred to as water conservation. Like many
water wholesalers, the Water District promotes water conservation. There are many ways the Water
District promotes water conservation. The principal way is through the media including
print media, social media, radio and other formats. Other ways including rebates directly to
consumers for water saving devices such as weather sensitive sprinkler controllers and rain barrels.
The last way is through cost shares, rebates or grants to its water retailers.

WaterSmart does not sell to individual households, individual commercial customers,
individual industrial customers, or individual agricultural customers, but to water retailers only.
As mentioned, the Water District is a water wholesaler, not a water retailer, that sells water to water
retailers with only a de minimis amount sold to individual households, individual commercial
customers, individual industrial customers, and individual agricultural customers. The Water
District does not buy anything from WaterSmart. In the case of water retailers, the Water District
offers cost-sharing, rebates, or grants for products or services that water retailers may use if
products or services are "Qualified."

Water District staff (not the Board) sets the specifications for Qualified water conservation
products or services used by waters retailers. One example of a Qualified water conservation
product used by waters retailers is Automatic Meter Infrastructure ("AMI"). Retailers install AMIs
for individual households, individual commercial customers, individual industrial customers, and
individual agricultural customers. This allows them to see their water usage in real-time or near real
time.

Another example is the type of services that WaterSmart offers. WaterSmart is one of
several vendors that has services to "communicate with their consumers about the value of water,
how their water use compares to others, and how they can save money." Water retailers may
receive a cost share, rebate or grant for any product or services that are Qualified, subject to a broad
water conservation budget and policies set by the Water District Board. The Water District staff
(not the Water District Board) create a list of products that meet the specifications and thus
Qualified. Water retailers can choose to use the Water District list, or they generate their own list
that meets the specifications. Where water retailers generate their own list for an RFP, they usually

⁴ https://www.watersmart.com/

File No. I-20-025
Page No. 4

double check with Water District staff to be sure the product or service is Qualified. Water retailers
then follow their purchasing process. This is usually done by sending out an RFP. The Water
District is not involved in sending out RFPs nor selecting which Qualified product or services is
chosen. The Water District Board only votes on the general budget and policies for water
conservation. Funds are allocated pro-rata to water retailers based on criteria such as population,
water usage or other metrics. These metrics are set by Water District staff, not by the Water District
Board.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision would have a material
financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests distinguishable from the
decision's effect on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) As pertinent to your request,
an official's interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are set
forth in Section 87103 and include:

• A business interest in any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect
investment worth $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)), or in which the official is a director, officer,
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any management position (Section 87103(d)).

• A source of income interest in any source from whom the official receives $500 or more in
value in the 12 months prior to when the decision is made including a pro rata share of income of
any business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly or
beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater. (Sections 82030 and 87103(c).)

• An interest in the official's personal finances including those of immediate family
members. (See Section 87103.)⁵

Although you state that Boardmember Kremen "believes his current stake is worthless,"
Boardmember Kremen must make a good faith and reasonable estimate of the value of the
investment. To the extent this investment in WaterSmart is valued at $2,000 or more, or he becomes
a director or officer of WaterSmart, Boardmember Kremen has an interest in WaterSmart as a
business entity. In addition, while he currently receives no income from WaterSmart, he would have
an interest in WaterSmart as a source of income should he receive $500 or more in the 12 months
prior to a decision that may implicate his interests. However, should he continue to have less than a
10-percent interest in WaterSmart, he will not have a source of income interest in any of its clients.

We note that we proceed with our analysis under the Act assuming Boardmember Kremen
has an interest in WaterSmart as a business entity or source of income. If he does not have an
interest under the Act, the Act would not prohibit him from taking part in the decisions. However,
his existing interest in WaterSmart may still be an interest for purposes of Section 1090, even if his
stake is valued at less than $2,000. Accordingly, the Water District and Boardmember Kremen

⁵ Regulation 18702.5(c) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's interest in his or her personal
finances is not considered separately if the decision affects the official's business interest or real property interest.
Accordingly, we do not analyze any effect on your personal finances further.

File No. I-20-025
Page No. 5

should seek additional advice under Section 1090 before entering any contract that may implicate
WaterSmart. In seeking advice under Section 1090, the Water District and Boardmember Kremen
will need to identify the specific contract and the nature of the contract.

Foreseeability and Materiality

Regulation 18701(a) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's financial
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in
the decision. An official's interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is a named party in, or the
subject of, the decision. An interest is the "subject of a proceeding" if the decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the interest including any decision affecting an interest in real property as described
in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1) through (6).

Regulation 18701(b) sets forth the foreseeability standard applicable to a decision's effect
on an official's interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, and provides that the effect on
such an interest is reasonably foreseeable if it "can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more
than hypothetical or theoretical."

Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to a decision's reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on an official's source of income interest. Under that regulation and
that regulation, Boardmember Kremen is prohibited from taking part in decisions where
WaterSmart is a named party in, or subject of, the decision because it is reasonably foreseeable that
those decisions would have a material financial effect on his source of income interest in
WaterSmart. Subdivision (a)(4), in turn, prohibits him from taking part in decisions where
WaterSmart would be financially affected under the materiality standards in Regulation 18702.1.

Under Regulation 18702.1,6 a reasonably foreseeable effect is material anytime an interest
in a business entity is explicitly involved in the decision. For a business entity not explicitly
involved in the decision, Regulation 18702.1(a)(2) provides that the decision's reasonably
foreseeable financial effect is material if the decision may result in an increase or decrease of the
business's annual gross revenues, or the value of the business's assets or liabilities, in an amount
equal to or more than $1,000,000, or five percent of the business's annual gross revenues and the
increase or decrease is at least $10,000.7 A reasonably foreseeable financial effect is also considered
material if the decision may cause the business entity to incur or avoid additional expenses or to
reduce or eliminate expenses in an amount equal to or more than $250,000 or one percent of the
entity's annual gross revenues and the change in expenses is at least $2,500. (Regulation
18702.1(a)(3).)

Therefore, the Act would prohibit Boardmember Kremen from taking part in a Water
District decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that decision would have a material financial effect

6 Please note that Regulation 18702.1 was recently amended.

7 Note the exception under subdivision (b) for small shareholders whose only interest in the entity is an
investment interest with a value of $25,000 or less (and less than one percent of the entity's shares). In that situation, the
decision's effect on the official's investment interest in the entity is only material under subdivisions (a)(2)-(3) or
(a)(4)(B) of Regulation 18702.1.

File No. I-20-025
Page No. 6

on his source of income interest in WaterSmart under Regulation 18702.3(a)(2) or Regulation
18702.1.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel


By:    Jack Woodside
Jack Woodside
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

JW:aja

Do the conflict of interest provisions under the Act prohibit Boardmember Kremen from 
having an interest in WaterSmart Software, LLC, including possibly serving on its board, while 
simultaneously serving on the board of the Santa Clara Valley Water District (“Water District”)?

decision. Generally, we can only caution that the Act may prohibit him from taking part in such 
decisions if he has a potentially disqualifying economic interest under the Act.

sections.analysis:
The Act’s conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a 
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision would have a material 
financial effect on one or more of the official’s financial interests distinguishable from the 
decision’s effect on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) As pertinent to your request, 
an official’s interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are set 
forth in Section 87103 and include: 
 
• A business interest in any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect 
investment worth $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)), or in which the official is a director, officer, 
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any management position (Section 87103(d)). 
  
• A source of income interest in any source from whom the official receives $500 or more in 
value in the 12 months prior to when the decision is made including a pro rata share of income of 
any business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly or 
beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater. (Sections 82030 and 87103(c).) 
  
• An interest in the official’s personal finances including those of immediate family 
members. (See Section 87103.)5 
  
Although you state that Boardmember Kremen “believes his current stake is worthless,” 
Boardmember Kremen must make a good faith and reasonable estimate of the value of the 
investment. To the extent his investment in WaterSmart is valued at $2,000 or more, or he becomes 
a director or officer of WaterSmart, Boardmember Kremen has an interest in WaterSmart as a 
business entity. In addition, while he currently receives no income from WaterSmart, he would have 
an interest in WaterSmart as a source of income should he receive $500 or more in the 12 months 
prior to a decision that may implicate his interests. However, should he continue to have less than a 
10-percent interest in WaterSmart, he will not have a source of income interest in any of its clients.  
 
We note that we proceed with our analysis under the Act assuming Boardmember Kremen 
has an interest in WaterSmart as a business entity or source of income. If he does not have an 
interest under the Act, the Act would not prohibit him from taking part in the decisions. However, 
his existing interest in WaterSmart may still be an interest for purposes of Section 1090, even if his 
stake is valued at less than $2,000. Accordingly, the Water District and Boardmember Kremen 
 
5 Regulation 18702.5(c) provides that a decision’s financial effect on an official’s interest in his or her personal 
finances is not considered separately if the decision affects the official’s business interest or real property interest. 
Accordingly, we do not analyze any effect on your personal finances further.    
 
 
should seek additional advice under Section 1090 before entering any contract that may implicate 
WaterSmart. In seeking advice under Section 1090, the Water District and Boardmember Kremen 
will need to identify the specific contract and the nature of the contract. 
   
Foreseeability and Materiality 
  
Regulation 18701(a) provides that a decision’s financial effect on an official’s financial 
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official’s interest is “explicitly involved” in 
the decision. An official’s interest is “explicitly involved” if the interest is a named party in, or the 
subject of, the decision. An interest is the “subject of a proceeding” if the decision involves the 
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or 
contract with, the interest including any decision affecting an interest in real property as described 
in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1) through (6). 
  
Regulation 18701(b) sets forth the foreseeability standard applicable to a decision’s effect 
on an official’s interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, and provides that the effect on 
such an interest is reasonably foreseeable if it “can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more 
than hypothetical or theoretical.” 
 
Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to a decision’s reasonably 
foreseeable financial effect on an official’s source of income interest. Under subdivision (a)(1) of 
that regulation, Boardmember Kremen is prohibited from taking part in decisions where 
WaterSmart is a named party in, or subject of, the decision because it is reasonably foreseeable that 
those decisions would have a material financial effect on his source of income interest in 
WaterSmart. Subdivision (a)(4), in turn, prohibits him from taking part in decisions where 
WaterSmart would be financially affected under the materiality standards in Regulation 18702.1.  
 
Under Regulation 18702.1,6 a reasonably foreseeable effect is material anytime an interest 
in a business entity is explicitly involved in the decision. For a business entity not explicitly 
involved in the decision, Regulation 18702.1(a)(2) provides that the decision’s reasonably 
foreseeable financial effect is material if the decision may result in an increase or decrease of the 
business’s annual gross revenues, or the value of the business’s assets or liabilities, in an amount 
equal to or more than $1,000,000, or five percent of the business’s annual gross revenues and the 
increase or decrease is at least $10,000.7 A reasonably foreseeable financial effect is also considered 
material if the decision may cause the business entity to incur or avoid additional expenses or to 
reduce or eliminate expenses in an amount equal to or more than $250,000 or one percent of the 
entity’s annual gross revenues and the change in expenses is at least $2,500. (Regulation 
18702.1(a)(3).) 
 
 Therefore, the Act would prohibit Boardmember Kremen from taking part in a Water 
District decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that decision would have a material financial effect 
 
6 Please note that Regulation 18702.1 was recently amended.   
  
7 Note the exception under subdivision (b) for small shareholders whose only interest in the entity is an 
investment interest with a value of $25,000 or less (and less than one percent of the entity’s shares). In that situation, the 
decision’s effect on the official’s investment interest in the entity is only material under subdivisions (a)(2)-(3) or 
(a)(4)(B) of Regulation 18702.1.   
 
 
on his source of income interest in WaterSmart under Regulation 18702.3(a)(2) or Regulation 
18702.1.
